author: @viviennehateley1843

author: @OldMysticFantasist
text: My father (a recreational pilot of 1 to 4 seater single-engine prop planes) was fond of telling the story of the man who, when asked "why would he decide to do such a stupid thing as jump out of his airplane without a parachute?", replied "Well the plane, including the parachute, was on fire, so at the time that seemed the thing to do."  🤔

author: @destrollip506
text: Thank you for posting this video, I’ve shared it with all my staff.

author: @gamble6363
text: Always good to hear and remind yourself. I think alot of us today expect things and results too fast and get put off  when it isn't easy.

author: @ollieb9875
text: Bring an autodidact has pros and cons I suppose, and I agree one shouldn't be criticised for giving it a go!

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text:
When something go's wrong it is only a fail if you learn nothing from it. You only fail when you give up. Until then its an educational experience. Possibly an expensive one.. but still a lesson. Self made millionaires are supposed to fail on average 7 times before they succeed.  So somebody calculated a long time ago. So if your 7th business becomes unprofitable.. go again but learn from all 7 learning experiences and sit down at the end of each one and work out why and where things did not work out.. a debriefing session in military language. 
The key advice I can give is above with the added  point of always keep smiling! No one wants to deal with a misery guts.. or work with them. :)

author: @ianbottom7396
text:
As someone with several trades and over 35 years experience, owning cnc plasma cutter, a decent laser cutter and several 3D printers I can categorically say it’s not always the first build of anything that is 100% right, there are always things that firstly could be done better but secondly done completely different to another persons approach, that doesn’t make it wrong, we are all humans and diverse in our experiences and priorities, welding is a great example of this. I’m not a welder by trade but pretty handy with TIG and stick and I’ve always loved welding and done quite a lot over the years and always tried to do a good looking, strong weld but having been doing it in my business for the past 5 years, I’m not quite as pedantic, you soon realise it’s not how it looks but if it’s fit for purpose and unless you are engaged in aerospace, high pressure pipe work etc quite often welding doesn’t need to be done to a high standard UNLESS the application requires it.

Robert I don’t always agree with how or why you do what you do but by and large I enjoy watching your videos and I thank you and many others who make good video content, I appreciate it as I learn skills or am inspired to start another project of my own. 
Don’t take too much criticism to  heart Robert because firstly you’re working with what you have or found and secondly I’m sure you inspire many to have a crack at something they never would have without guys like yourself so please carry on 👍

author: @teltwosheds2972
text: Well put👍👏

author: @samanderson4881
text: very motivaitonal!

author: @zaneaussie
text: Happy new years Rob! Hope you have had a nice Christmas! I have been going over a lot of your older videos lately to try and understand battery electrolyte a bit better! Not sure if you recall but I requested a video on solid state batteries but I am wondering what role the electrolyte actually has? Does it increase the amount of charge held by the battery at all? Or is it there solely to act as a conductive medium between the two electrodes?

author: @samuelthomasengardio
text: I have tons of respect for those that come up with theories and pursue them. I've spend alot of thought and time on theories that were wrong.  Projects that involved thousands of custom pieces and tons of money, all I'd recieve was a goofy looking piece of artwork that I'd immediately dismantle and dispose of. Next....

author: @tcoo1999
text: Nice one Robert

author: @Gwydion67
text:
Pew, I'm so glad that this for sure is the one thing I'm good at:
Being happy whenever I achieved something, being patient with myself if it didn't work out or wasn't the result I was hoping for. And yes, *IF* something went well, I even allow me being a bit of proud.
With this satisfying experience I'm perfectly motivated to compliment others as well. 🥰

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Words of wisdom :-). I'm a lot like you these days in when it comes to arrogance and being stubborn. It pays off often enough. This was not always the case. I guess when one is young it's different. 
I hope you do not see my comments as negative. These are intended to be constructive.

author: @whosle
text: Good video!  I enjoy criticism of my work as long as it is constructive and helps me improve. A majority of criticism comes from those you should ignore. Sometimes it is tough to tell the difference , so your own failure(s) may help lead a way to better understanding

author: @Mesco-yw9gi
text:
Good judgment comes from experience, and a lot of that comes from bad judgment.

Will Rogers

author: @Beamer1969
text:
Good decisions come from experience.
Experience comes from bad decisions.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I think the best thing that can happen to you is to make a mistake or fail.  You will learn sooooo much more in terms of techniques, solutions, etc than if everything had simply worked out as planned.  If you're not regularly making mistakes or encountering failure, you're not learning much (and probably not doing much).  "Success" isn't a product of the absence of mistakes, it's the product of persistence in spite of them.

author: @victoralosi1461
text: It takes courage to try and progress on what level your at .  Love it  do it

author: @tomjones3729
text: Think you so much For your Humble approach Andrew more humbling conversation maybe trolls go fornicate their hat briskly And thank you so much

author: @sylvanenergy
text: Cheers Old Boy!

author: @anneinfurna8528
text: One thing that is tough when you are a "Perfectionist", is understanding that often times things need to be workable, with not necessarily the spit and polish of a production piece. Concept is more important to proof out an idea, but I find if you are not careful, you get lost in the details and the detailing, and lose interest when it 'Doesn't look as good' as you want it to. Discouragement is the other guys defence when They do not have the skills to even imagine something to fruition, and its used to deaden your 'spark' as it were. I personally Thrive on someone's Naysaying on any of my projects, as it tends to egg me on, to show them...You Know?  If I could instill one thing in anyone contemplating making something, i say Do not let others rule your thoughts and actions, Cut your own Groove. Would you agree?

author: @jonnyswalk_7
text: Always wise and encouraging words Robert - Thank you so much for sharing 😊 👍 - Jonathan - South Wales 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁷󠁬󠁳󠁿

author: @edkoetsier339
text: Learn by doing. Always. It's more important to make an attempt. If you don't attempt you never learn. There is no law against multi iterations.

author: @ivanolsen8596
text:
Very wise words, Robert not only you are a skilled inventor, developer,
tradesman and thinker, you also make a great philosopher.

author: @brianmcnish1835
text: Really wish I could like this 100 times!!!

author: @nil5221
text: When you have a go, watch some videos, develop some skill, and have high standards of workmanship, a weird thing happens: you pay a professional to get a job done quickly, then have to go back and re-do it yourself...or at least, those bits which don't meet your standard. Yes it takes a lot longer, but you won't be looking at a wonky "good enough"  job for the rest of your life. Even the professionals don't necessarily do the "best" job. DIY if possible...and keep doing it.

author: @Edsbar
text: The materialistic among us may rubbish our achievement but they will never know that the real achievement is the learning and that the thing we made is merely a by-product of that.

author: @beamer.electronics
text: Interesting, thank you. I've designed and built some very sophisticated equipment in my time. However, I now (in retirement and health not so good), enjoy building electronic ideas into upcycled items for others to learn from. These I present on YT and in a sense, I've now moved backwards - creating complexity into something that, at times, looks like rubbish (literally ;)

author: @nicoeggers9445

author: @FlyingFun.
text:
Yep, if you dont try you wont ever learn.
Some constructive criticism can of course be good but needs to be worded carefully in a positive way explaining the  details but like you say its generally not needed because we can learn.from our mistakes more than we can from just doing it right first go.
If something is dangerous it's worth pointing out to stop someone getting hurt though.

I love passing on knowledge but try to guide a student rather than just show them , sometimes they try something different and it works really well.
Thumbs up given again.

author: @rodryk5605

author: @totherarf
text:
It takes 10,000 hrs to be come an Expert in a skill!
There are few who will spend that amount of time doing it, most will be happy with being Good at something.

When I think about it the best words of encouragement came from my flight instructor .... "Dave, if it were easy Everyone would be doing it!" ..... He was right too ;0)

author: @xspotbox4400
text:
But seriously, this channel is probably closest to our common steam punk future things could be, if we want to change our global consumer habits. It would be like in FRP games, every person, house and stuff at least partially unique and long lasting, but most of all, adjusted to individual daily needs. But turning world into Indian or African junk yard is also not a good idea, it would take some new global ethics to make people believe this is actually best way to live and there are so many things in existence that they doesn't really matter. Once people would became self sufficient and live like astronauts on a foreign world, cities and their population would became as technological temples, ever more detached from the rest of biological habitat. Or it could turn into a Mad Max kind of dystopia, simply because human condition includes something called dumb, but though and with good in it's hearth. 
They don't give out those Darwin awards on that internet site for nothing.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Whenever I hear someone say something like this I wonder:
Why didn't the wood shop and metal shop teachers in high school say that to their students on a regular basis.
If I had heard that; I might have taken more than one semester of those classes, but given the fact that I was so obviously miserable at those things; I chose to not pursue them.

Interesting observation: Most of the projects ended up being successful both technically and financially!

author: @69waveydavey
text: There's a great imbalance of talkers as opposed to do-ers these days. I'm about the same age as you Rob and have noticed the increase as I've got older. I come from a northern industrial town where, as a kid, everybody made something, now they just sit at desks and talk everything.

replies.author: @69waveydavey

author: @jimmycorkhill1390
text: Good stuff. I would add to that the key element of emotion. We are in an age of hyper sensitivity and fear. If it is mere name calling then ignore it or make them see the error of their ways. Best wishes.

author: @peterjames2117
text: Thanks for this Rob. I totally agree with your approach. I always try to encourage my students to think of design as an iterative process - the more times you make something, the more you learn and the better it gets. Often the best ideas grow out of making mistakes and finding things out by accident.

The important lesson to learn from the best of your ability is that ability improves the more you do it, like welding Robert. LOL.

Reminds me of a Confucius saying, he who walks through airport turnstiles sideways is often going to Bangkok.
